message:
fix(packs/dns): match dig AXFR/IXFR case-insensitively

`dig` accepts query types in any case — `dig AXFR example.com`
and `dig axfr example.com` are equivalent on every dig
implementation. The DNS pack's destructive pattern only matched
the lowercase form:

    r"\bdig\b.*\b(?:axfr|ixfr)\b"

…so an attacker (or a typo-in-uppercase user, or any tooling
that emits the canonical uppercase RFC names) could run a zone
transfer past the guard. The matching safe pattern had the
mirror bug — its negative lookahead also only watched for
lowercase, so `dig AXFR ...` would also satisfy the safe matcher
and, before the segment-aware fix landed earlier today, that
would have masked the destructive scan entirely.

Two changes:

1. **Destructive pattern.** Wrapped the entire pattern in
   `(?i: ... )` so `\bdig\b.*\b(?:axfr|ixfr)\b` is matched
   case-insensitively. Now `dig AXFR`, `dig Axfr`, `DIG axfr`,
   etc. all hit the rule. Same severity (Medium), same reason
   text, same explanation.

2. **Safe pattern.** Tightened the negative lookahead to use a
   case-insensitive group `(?i:\b(?:axfr|ixfr)\b)`. Before, the
   lookahead said "no lowercase axfr|ixfr"; now it says "no
   axfr|ixfr in any case". The outer `\bdig\b` stays
   case-sensitive because real-world dig invocations are
   lowercase by convention and case-folding the command name
   would risk masking unrelated commands that happen to spell
   "DIG" (e.g. as part of a longer identifier — `\b` already
   prevents that, but keeping the outer pattern case-sensitive
   matches the rest of the safe-pattern corpus's style).

   The two negative lookaheads (`(?!.*\baxfr\b)(?!.*\bixfr\b)`)
   were also collapsed into a single `(?!.*(?i:\b(?:axfr|ixfr)\b))`
   for symmetry with the destructive pattern. Both forms are
   semantically equivalent on this pattern (they exclude any
   tail containing AXFR or IXFR); the unified form is just
   easier to keep in sync if a future RFC adds a third
   transfer type.

3. **Tests.** Mirror-tested the new positive cases:
   - `dig AXFR example.com` → blocked with `dns-dig-zone-transfer`
   - `dig IXFR=12345 example.com` → blocked
   - Severity test: `dig AXFR example.com` → Medium
   The existing lowercase tests stay unchanged so we don't
   regress the original coverage.

No semantic change for the lowercase-only path: every test that
passed before still passes. The new path catches the
uppercase/mixed-case variants the pack was previously missing.
